topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Eleanor Vance stared at the object resting on her mahogany desk. It was a gaudy, oversized novelty keychain shaped like a cartoon cat wearing sunglasses, complete with a cheap plastic chain. The envelope, thick and cream-colored, bore the familiar, looping script of Julian Thorne. Eleanor picked it up with the tips of her fingers, the texture of the paper feeling offensively slick. Her jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. She remembered Julian, the student who had consistently submitted sloppy work and whose incessant, poorly phrased questions had often grated on her nerves. The sheer audacity of the gesture, the childishness of the trinket, made a strange, tight knot form beneath her ribs. She set the letter aside, her gaze fixed on the framed diploma on the wall, wishing the wood grain would swallow her whole.

[story 2]

"Oh, for heaven's sake," muttered Mr. Harrison, carefully placing the small, brightly colored box onto his filing cabinet. It was a personalized succulent, unnervingly vibrant, accompanied by a handwritten note from Sarah Jenkins. The handwriting was aggressively flourished, looping in ways that seemed deliberately theatrical. Harrison felt a wave of profound unease wash over him as he reread the message. It spoke of shared, bygone moments, moments he had tried very hard to compartmentalize and forget. He traced the edge of the paper with his thumb, his knuckles whitening slightly. The air in his office suddenly felt too thick, too charged, and he instinctively leaned back in his chair, creating a deliberate physical distance between himself and the unwelcome intrusion.

[story 3]

I picked up the package, my fingers brushing against the glossy wrapping paper. It was a collection of obscure, extremely niche vinyl records—music I had never heard of, and certainly never approved of. The sender was Mark Peterson, a student from my advanced literature seminar whom I had found relentlessly pretentious in his essays. When I tore open the package, the scent of old cardboard and something faintly metallic rose up. The records themselves were wrapped haphazardly, a clear indication of carelessness. I held the first record up to the light, the artwork jarring and overly dramatic. A slow, cold tremor ran through my hand as I realized the effort involved in this unsolicited offering. I carefully slid the entire collection into a drawer, the sound of the plastic sliding against the wood seeming deafening in the quiet room.
